glossary.definition: The brief delay before the AI starts typing its first word, mostly depending on how long your prompt is and server traffic.
The speed at which the AI generates text after it starts, determining how fast you get the full answer.
A cost-saving feature where the AI remembers a long set of instructions you send frequently, so you do not have to pay full price for it every time.

hook.scenario: Your RAG chatbot prototype works great. You show it to the team. A product manager asks: 'How much will this cost at 10,000 requests per day?' You realize you've never calculated it.
hook.question: How do you estimate and control the token cost and latency of an LLM call before it hits production?

mental_model.intuition: Every LLM call is a taxi ride: you pay per token (the meter), and the bigger the car (model tier), the higher the rate. Latency tracks output tokens per second — not model size alone.
mental_model.description: Cost = (prompt_tokens × input_price + completion_tokens × output_price) per million tokens. The two levers: model choice (gpt-4o-mini is ~15× cheaper per token than gpt-4o) and prompt size (smaller prompt + smaller output = linear cost reduction). Latency for LLMs is dominated by output tokens — generating more tokens takes more time regardless of prompt size. Time-to-first-token (TTFT) is a separate metric driven by server load and prompt prefill.

sections.body: The pricing formula is simple: cost = input_tokens × input_$/MTok + output_tokens × output_$/MTok. For gpt-4o (mid-2025 pricing): $2.50 per million input tokens, $10.00 per million output tokens. For gpt-4o-mini: $0.15 input / $0.60 output. A 2k-token prompt + 500-token reply on gpt-4o costs about $0.010. The same call on gpt-4o-mini costs about $0.0006. At 10k requests/day that's $100/day vs $6/day.
sections.recap: Cost = input tokens × rate + output tokens × rate. Output is priced 4× higher than input on most models.

sections.body: The single most impactful cost decision is model choice. The right model is the SMALLEST one that meets quality requirements for your task. Use gpt-4o-mini for classification, routing, extraction of well-defined fields, and short summarization. Reserve gpt-4o for complex reasoning, long-context tasks, and multimodal. A two-tier setup (mini for 80% of traffic, 4o for the hard 20%) typically cuts costs by 60–80%.
sections.recap: Model tier is the biggest lever. Mini for simple tasks; 4o for complex. Never default to 4o for everything.

sections.body: Latency has two components. Time-to-first-token (TTFT): the wait before any output arrives — driven by server load and prompt length (longer prompts take longer to prefill). Tokens-per-second (TPS): the speed of generation — roughly constant per model at a given server load, ~40–80 TPS for gpt-4o. Total latency ≈ TTFT + (output_tokens / TPS). Cutting max_tokens is the most direct latency lever you control.
sections.recap: Total latency = TTFT + output_tokens / speed. Limit output tokens to cut tail latency.

sections.body: Prompt compression is the overlooked lever. The most expensive token in a RAG prompt is the system prompt repeated on every call. If your system prompt is 1,000 tokens and you make 10k calls/day, that's 10M tokens of system-prompt cost alone. Techniques: compress the system prompt (fewer words, same intent), use prompt caching (OpenAI's cached input pricing is 50% off for repeated prefixes), and trim retrieved context aggressively.
sections.recap: Repeated system prompts are a hidden cost multiplier. Cache or compress them.

code_snippets.title: Estimate cost before sending a request
code_snippets.code:
import tiktoken

# Pricing (per million tokens, mid-2025)
PRICING = {
    'gpt-4o':       {'input': 2.50,  'output': 10.00},
    'gpt-4o-mini':  {'input': 0.15,  'output': 0.60},
}

def estimate_cost(messages: list[dict], expected_output_tokens: int, model='gpt-4o') -> float:
    enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model(model)
    input_tokens = sum(4 + len(enc.encode(m.get('content', ''))) for m in messages) + 2
    p = PRICING[model]
    cost = (input_tokens * p['input'] + expected_output_tokens * p['output']) / 1_000_000
    return cost

messages = [
    {'role': 'system', 'content': 'You are a helpful assistant.'},
    {'role': 'user',   'content': 'Summarise the following in 3 bullet points: ' + 'x ' * 500},
]
print(f'Estimated cost: ${estimate_cost(messages, 150, "gpt-4o"):.5f}')       # ~$0.0040
print(f'Estimated cost: ${estimate_cost(messages, 150, "gpt-4o-mini"):.5f}')  # ~$0.0002
code_snippets.explanation: Run this estimate before choosing a model. For a 1k-token prompt + 150-token reply, gpt-4o costs 20× more than gpt-4o-mini. If mini's quality is acceptable, use it. Build this into your observability pipeline to track actual vs estimated costs per request type.

code_snippets.title: Log actual cost per call from usage object
code_snippets.code:
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()

PRICING = {
    'gpt-4o':       {'input': 2.50, 'output': 10.00},
    'gpt-4o-mini':  {'input': 0.15, 'output': 0.60},
}

def call_and_log(messages: list[dict], model='gpt-4o-mini') -> str:
    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=model, messages=messages, max_tokens=300,
    )
    usage = resp.usage
    p = PRICING[model]
    cost = (usage.prompt_tokens * p['input'] +
            usage.completion_tokens * p['output']) / 1_000_000
    print(f'[cost] ${cost:.6f} | in={usage.prompt_tokens} out={usage.completion_tokens} | model={model}')
    return resp.choices[0].message.content
code_snippets.explanation: Log token counts and cost on every production call. This is how you catch runaway prompts: a spike in prompt_tokens means your RAG context is too large; a spike in completion_tokens means max_tokens isn't set. The usage object is the ground truth — always cheaper than Datadog metrics.

common_mistakes.title: Using gpt-4o for every task by default
common_mistakes.explanation: gpt-4o is 15–20× more expensive per token than gpt-4o-mini. For classification, extraction, and short summarization tasks, gpt-4o-mini achieves equivalent quality at a fraction of the cost. Benchmark both before defaulting to the expensive model.

common_mistakes.title: Not accounting for output token cost
common_mistakes.explanation: Output tokens are typically priced 4× higher than input tokens per million. A prompt that asks for a 2,000-word essay pays far more in output cost than in input cost. Setting max_tokens is both a latency and a cost control measure.

recall_questions.q: What are the two components of LLM API cost, and which is typically priced higher?
recall_questions.answer: Input tokens (prompt) and output tokens (reply). Output tokens are typically priced 4× higher than input tokens per million.

recall_questions.q: What is the primary latency lever you control as an application developer?
recall_questions.answer: max_tokens — limiting output length is the most direct way to reduce total generation latency, since latency scales linearly with output tokens generated.

recall_questions.q: Why is a repeated system prompt a hidden cost multiplier in high-volume applications?
recall_questions.answer: Every API call includes the full system prompt in the input tokens billed. At 10k calls/day, a 1,000-token system prompt alone costs 10M input tokens daily. Caching the prompt prefix (at 50% off) or compressing it reduces this directly.

oa_questions.question: Your team's LLM API bill doubles in a week with no change in user traffic. What are three things you check first?
oa_questions.answer: 1) prompt_tokens per call — did retrieved context size or system prompt size grow? 2) completion_tokens per call — was max_tokens removed or increased, causing longer outputs? 3) Model tier — did someone switch from gpt-4o-mini to gpt-4o for a high-volume endpoint? Log usage on every call to answer these instantly.
oa_questions.approach: Three levers: prompt size, output size, model tier. Frame it as a logging/observability gap — you can't debug cost spikes without per-call usage logging.

oa_questions.question: Design a cost-optimized pipeline for a support chatbot that handles 100k requests/day, where 75% are simple FAQs and 25% are complex multi-step questions.
oa_questions.answer: Two-tier routing: a cheap classifier (gpt-4o-mini, <50 tokens I/O) labels each request as 'simple' or 'complex'. Simple requests go to gpt-4o-mini with a short answer prompt (max_tokens=200). Complex requests go to gpt-4o with full RAG context. This routes 75k/day to mini (~$0.15/MTok) and 25k/day to gpt-4o (~$2.50/MTok), reducing overall cost by ~70% vs using gpt-4o for everything.
oa_questions.approach: The key insight is routing classification — using the cheapest model to triage which calls need the expensive model. Show the math: cost savings scale linearly with the fraction of calls routed to mini.
